mission.title: Install and Configure Kyverno (Policy Engine)
mission.description: Kyverno is a Kubernetes-native policy engine that allows users to manage security and compliance through policy-as-code. It is particularly useful for platform engineering teams looking to enforce policies across their Kubernetes clusters. Key trade-offs include the need for careful policy design and the potential complexity of managing multiple policies.

steps.title: Add the Kyverno Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official Kyverno Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration.

```bash

helm repo add kyverno https://kyverno.github.io/kyverno

```

> This command sets up the repository for installing Kyverno.

steps.title: Install Kyverno CRDs
steps.description:
Before installing Kyverno, we need to install its Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs).

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kyverno/kyverno/v1.17.1/config/release/kyverno-crds.yaml

```

> This command applies the CRDs required by Kyverno.

steps.title: Install Kyverno using Helm
steps.description:
This step installs Kyverno in the specified namespace using Helm.

```bash

helm install kyverno kyverno/kyverno --version 1.17.1 --namespace kyverno --create-namespace

```

> This command installs Kyverno in the 'kyverno' namespace.

steps.title: Verify Kyverno installation
steps.description:
After installation, we need to check that the Kyverno pods are running correctly.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n kyverno

```

> This command lists the pods in the Kyverno namespace to ensure they are running.

steps.title: Create an example policy
steps.description:
This step creates a sample policy to demonstrate Kyverno's capabilities.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-labels
spec:
  validationFailureAction: enforce
  rules:
    - name: check-labels
      match:
        resources:
          kinds:
            - Pod
      validate:
        message: 'Label `app` is required.'
        pattern:
          metadata:
            labels:
              app: '?'
EOF

```

> This command creates a policy that requires the 'app' label on Pods.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-labels
spec:
  validationFailureAction: enforce
  rules:
    - name: check-labels
      match:
        resources:
          kinds:
            - Pod
      validate:
        message: 'Label `app` is required.'
        pattern:
          metadata:
            labels:
              app: '?'
EOF

steps.title: Verify the policy is applied
steps.description:
This step checks if the policy we created is functioning as expected.

```bash

kubectl get cpol require-labels -o yaml

```

> This command retrieves the created ClusterPolicy to verify its configuration.

uninstall.title: Uninstall Kyverno
uninstall.description:
This step removes Kyverno and its associated resources. Be aware that any policies created will be lost.

```bash

helm uninstall kyverno --namespace kyverno

kubectl delete -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kyverno/kyverno/v1.17.1/config/release/kyverno-crds.yaml

```

> This command uninstalls Kyverno from the specified namespace.
> This command removes the CRDs associated with Kyverno.

upgrade.title: Upgrade Kyverno
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you back up your policies. This step includes draining the pods if necessary, upgrading, and verifying the installation.

```bash

kubectl get cpol -o yaml > kyverno-policies-backup.yaml

helm upgrade kyverno kyverno/kyverno --version 1.17.1 --namespace kyverno

kubectl get pods -n kyverno

```

> Backup existing policies to a file.
> This command upgrades Kyverno to the specified version.
> Verify that the Kyverno pods are running after the upgrade.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'validation failed: missing required field'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This error occurs when a resource does not meet the validation criteria defined in the policy.

**Fix:**
Ensure that the resource being created or updated includes all required fields as specified in the policy.

troubleshooting.title: Kyverno pod is in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This can happen due to misconfiguration in the policies or insufficient resources allocated to the Kyverno pod.

**Fix:**
Check the logs of the Kyverno pod using 'kubectl logs <pod-name> -n kyverno' and adjust the configuration or resources accordingly.

troubleshooting.title: Policy not enforcing as expected
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The policy might not be correctly applied or the validationFailureAction is not set to enforce.

**Fix:**
Verify the policy configuration and ensure that 'validationFailureAction' is set to 'enforce'.

troubleshooting.title: Unable to create resources due to policy violation
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The resource being created does not comply with the defined policies.

**Fix:**
Review the policy requirements and modify the resource to comply with the policies.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for new policy types and enhanced validation features.
versionNotes.deprecations: No specific deprecations in this version.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: No specific migration steps required for this version.

resolution.summary: You should see the Kyverno pods running in the 'kyverno' namespace with a status of 'Running'. Use 'kubectl get pods -n kyverno' to verify.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with the necessary tools installed.
